retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The gross pathological, histochemical, light microscopic, and ultrastructural findings vary with coronary occlusion and ischemia onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The three components in the evaluation of the MI are clinical features, ECG findings, and cardiac biomarkers.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The primary sequence of events in myocardial infarction involves hemorrhagic myocardial necrosis, followed by contraction band necrosis, and eventually, delayed inflammation and repair.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The thrombus formed at the site of plaque rupture in anterior myocardial infarction (MI) typically exhibits a white appearance (platelet-rich) at the site of rupture while appearing red at the margins (composed of red blood cells and fibrin). Thrombus formation involves a complex interplay of different cellular and non-cellular components within the blood. The breakdown of the extracellular matrix is a key factor in the exact cause of plaque rupture, with matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) playing a significant role.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Both myocardial infarction and silent myocardial ischemia involve hypoxic damage. However, myocardial infarction's histopathological features typically reflect more severe and irreversible tissue damage compared to silent myocardial ischemia, which may exhibit milder and more localized changes.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial infarction, in general, can be classified into types 1 to 5 based on etiology and pathogenesis. [4] Recent advances in cardiovascular imaging, revised ECG criteria, and high-sensitivity cardiovascular biomarker assays have enhanced the ability to classify the different causes of myocardial infarction more precisely. The underlying pathophysiology of type 1 myocardial infarction differs from that of types 2 through 5. Type 1 myocardial infarction is primarily associated with intracoronary atherothrombosis, whereas the other types may result from various mechanisms, some of which may involve an atherosclerotic component. Type 2 myocardial infarction is characterized by an imbalance in myocardial oxygen supply and demand unrelated to acute coronary atherothrombosis. Types 1 and 2 occur spontaneously, while types 4 and 5 are linked to medical procedures. Type 3 is only identified postmortem. Most types 1 and 2 myocardial infarction cases present as NSTEMI, but some manifest as STEMI (see Image . Acute Myocardial Infarction Types). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histological findings become evident if the ischemic state remains long enough to initiate myocardial damage. The following findings may be appreciated in the setting of acute myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Etiology -- Plaque Erosion
retrieved_docs.content: The thrombus appears white and contains abundant neutrophils, while smooth muscle cells are interspersed. The necrotic core is small, and there is a lack of endothelial lining due to apoptosis. This allows the blood components to encounter the collagen, leading to thrombus formation.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Subsequently, nuclei reduction occurs between 1 to 3 days, and then, within the next 3 to 7 days, macrophages emerge to clear apoptotic cells. Granulation tissue appears at 7 to 10 days, and collagen type I deposition occurs after 10 days. Ultimately, after 2 months, the myocardium undergoes scarring.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Myocardial Infarction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Myocardial infarction results from decreased blood flow to myocardial tissue. The most common type of myocardial infarction, type 1 MI, is due to the rupture of a vulnerable atherosclerotic plaque comprising of macrophages, smooth muscle proliferation, and lipid-laden foamy cells that form on an injured vascular wall. When there is complete or near-complete occlusion of the vessel, there is little to no perfusion of the affected myocardium. This perturbed blood flow leads to ischemia, which has downstream effects of metabolic and ionic disturbances in the myocardium. Prolonged ischemia leads to apoptosis and necrosis of cardiomyocytes and cell death. Healing occurs through scar formation and is dependent upon an inflammatory cascade, which is triggered by the release of cytokines from dying cells — infiltrating phagocytes clear dead cells and matrix debris. The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system is activated. Transforming growth factor-beta is released and triggers the conversion of fibroblasts to myofibroblasts and the deposition of extracellular matrix proteins. The subsequent remodeling ultimately leads to dilation, scar formation, and progressive dysfunction. [2] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The acute occlusion of one or multiple large epicardial coronary arteries for more than 20 to 40 minutes can lead to acute myocardial infarction. The occlusion is usually thrombotic and due to the rupture of a plaque formed in the coronary arteries. The occlusion leads to a lack of oxygen in the myocardium, which results in sarcolemmal disruption and myofibril relaxation. [2] These changes are one of the first ultrastructural changes in the process of MI, which are followed by mitochondrial alterations. The prolonged ischemia ultimately results in liquefactive necrosis of myocardial tissue. The necrosis spreads from sub-endocardium to sub-epicardium. The subepicardium is believed to have increased collateral circulation, which delays its death. [2] Depending on the territory affected by the infarction, the cardiac function is compromised. Due to the negligible regeneration capacity of the myocardium, the infarcted area heals by scar formation, and often, the heart is remodeled characterized by dilation, segmental hypertrophy of remaining viable tissue, and cardiac dysfunction. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: "Cardiac enzymes" is a broad term encompassing several intracellular myocyte components that can be found in serum and measured under certain circumstances such as myocardial ischemia, trauma, myocarditis. In the proper clinical setting, elevation in the level of enzymes present in serum is key in the diagnosis of myocardial infarction. While troponin is the most commonly used cardiac enzyme for diagnosis of myocardial infarction, others exist and may be helpful in some situations.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The histological changes observed in MI evolve throughout the disease course. At time 0, no microscopic histologic changes are observed. Within 0.5 to 4 hours, light microscopy reveals the waviness of fibers at the tissue periphery along with glycogen depletion. At 4 to 12 hours, the myocardium undergoes coagulation necrosis and edema. At 12 to 24 hours, the gross specimen appears dark and mottled, with contraction band necrosis and neutrophil predominance observed on histopathology.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The rupture of an atherosclerotic plaque initiates an inflammatory response of monocytes and macrophages, leading to thrombus formation and platelet aggregation. This process decreases oxygen delivery through the coronary artery, resulting in inadequate oxygenation of the myocardium (see Image. Specimen Showing MI). The subsequent inability to produce ATP in the mitochondria triggers an ischemic cascade, ultimately leading to apoptosis (cell death) of the endocardium or myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Gross alterations become evident after 6 to 12 hours. Gross pathology reveals evidence of transmural infarction or myocardial necrosis in most cases of occlusion myocardial infarction (OMI). However, within hours of infarction, histo-chemically, the necrotic area appears pale with triphenyl tetrazolium chloride, indicating a lack of staining, whereas the uninfarcted area appears brick red. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: tissue in the pathophysiology and evaluation of AMI. Morphologically, acute infarct tissue links postoperative acute myocardial infarction and anterior non‑ST‑segment‑elevation myocardial infarction, indicating shared pathological changes across these clinical presentations. Acute subendocardial infarction is classified under “other specified forms of acute ischemic heart disease,” connecting it to a broader group of acute ischemic cardiac conditions. Together, these relationships underscore that coronary artery obstruction at diverse arterial segments is the primary mechanism driving myocardial damage, and that the common histopathology and diagnostic classification of the various AMI phenotypes are essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 975
retrieved_docs.content: artery, intermediate artery). Despite these variations in presentation and vascular involvement, the pathological finding site for all of them is the myocardial tissue, underscoring that myocardial injury is the central feature of acute coronary syndromes regardless of the precise ECG morphology or culprit vessel. Recognizing the specific type of infarction and its associated arterial occlusion aids clinicians in tailoring reperfusion strategies and optimizing patient care while acknowledging that the underlying myocardial damage remains the pivotal concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2367
retrieved_docs.content: myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, indicating that the broader category includes infarcts defined simply by the occluded major coronary vessel. The classification further extends to Acute myocardial infarction during procedure, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), and Acute widespread myocardial infarction, demonstrating that Acute infarct also captures procedural‑related, non‑ST‑segment elevation, and extensive myocardial injury patterns. Recognizing these diverse entities under the shared morphology of Acute infarct aids clinicians in precise diagnosis, risk stratification, and selection of targeted therapeutic strategies for patients presenting with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2364
retrieved_docs.content: coronary artery, and Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal portion of the right coronary artery all share the acute infarct morphology. In addition, acute infarct morphology is also associated with Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Together, these relationships illustrate that acute infarct serves as the common pathological substrate linking diverse coronary occlusion sites and both ST‑segment elevation and non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction presentations, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

Collectively, the interconnections among acute myocardial infarction, various forms of heart muscle injury, coronary arterial thrombotic events, associated arrhythmias, and related systemic conditions illustrate the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 971
retrieved_docs.content: The condition termed Acute non‑Q wave infarction – anterolateral represents a specific form of Non‑Q wave myocardial infarction and is also classified as a subtype of acute myocardial infarction. This infarction localizes to the Structure of myocardium of anterolateral region, an anatomical segment that forms part of the broader Myocardium and Cardiac Conduction System. Acute myocardial infarction is closely linked to the physiological process described as احْتِشاءُ عَضَلِ القَلْبِ (construction of the heart muscle), and the spectrum of the disease encompasses حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية (SMQ) (arterial thrombosis and embolism cases). These arterial thrombotic and embolic events are considered within the overall context of heart‑muscle pathology and are clinically associated with رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد (vascular rupture observable through the skin). Understanding how the anterolateral myocardial structure, the broader myocardial‑conduction system, and the underlying mechanisms of heart‑muscle formation and vascular injury interrelate is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted management of patients presenting with acute non‑Q wave infarction and related cardiovascular complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 976
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to these STEMI morphologies, Acute infarct also includes Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction during procedure, Acute widespread myocardial infarction, Postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Recognizing that all of these distinct anatomic and clinical variants share the overarching concept of acute infarct helps clinicians accurately classify, risk‑stratify, and manage patients presenting with acute coronary syndromes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct serves as the underlying morphological pattern that links a wide spectrum of acute myocardial infarction presentations. It is the associated morphology of classic Acute Q wave myocardial infarction and of Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder). Specific forms of ST‑segment elevation infarction—including those due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, involvement of the left anterior descending coronary artery in general, occlusion of the distal portion of the right coronary artery, occlusion of the intermediate artery, and occlusion of the posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery—are all described as having the morphology of an acute infarct. Likewise, acute infarct is the associated morphology of acute myocardial infarction caused by left coronary artery occlusion, by occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, and by right coronary artery occlusion. Non‑ST‑segment elevation presentations are also encompassed, with Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), Postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction all sharing the acute infarct morphology. Together, these relationships illustrate that regardless of the specific coronary artery involved, the location of the occlusion (proximal, mid, distal) or the clinical context (postoperative, subsequent event), the fundamental pathological substrate is an acute infarct. Recognizing this common morphological basis aids clinicians in categorizing myocardial infarctions, guiding diagnostic interpretation, and informing therapeutic strategies across diverse clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2272
retrieved_docs.content: elevation myocardial infarction, as well as procedural infarctions (Acute myocardial infarction during procedure) and infarctions specifically attributed to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery (Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of circumflex branch of left coronary artery). Recognizing that all of these entities share the common morphological classification of Acute infarct helps clinicians categorize the type, location, and context of myocardial injury, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct serves as a unifying morphological category that encompasses a wide spectrum of specific myocardial injury patterns. Within this category, Acute Q wave myocardial infarction is recognized as a distinct form of acute infarct. A broad range of Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) are also classified under acute infarct, each defined by the precise coronary artery segment that is occluded. These include Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the diagonal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the distal portion of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the intermediate artery, due to occlusion of the mid portion of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the proximal portion of the right coronary artery, and Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery. In addition, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery is another specific morphology linked to acute infarct. More extensive presentations such as Acute widespread myocardial infarction and postoperative forms like Postoperative acute myocardial infarction are also captured within the acute infarct morphology. Together, these entities illustrate how acute infarct integrates diverse anatomic and clinical variants of myocardial infarction, aiding clinicians in precise diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted management of patients with acute coronary occlusion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2016
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a broad morphological category that encompasses a variety of specific myocardial injury patterns. Within this category, acute Q wave infarction – widespread and acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread are recognized as distinct ECG‑based morphologies. Acute infarct also includes acute myocardial infarctions that arise from occlusion of particular coronary artery segments, such as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal, mid, or proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion; occlusion of the diagonal branch of the anterior descending artery; occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery; and occlusion of the right coronary artery at its proximal, mid, distal, or marginal branches. Additional associated morphologies are acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedural event. Together, these entities define the spectrum of acute infarct morphologies, linking the anatomical site of coronary obstruction with the resulting electrocardiographic and clinical presentation, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.content: proximal, mid, distal, intermediate, posterior‑lateral branch, left anterior descending), each condition reflects acute injury to myocardial tissue. Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal finding site across these varied presentations aids clinicians in correlating ECG findings, angiographic territories, and clinical contexts to guide diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic interventions.
